explanation_md: `hello` is declared `virtual` in `Base`. When `call` receives a `Derived` object as a `const Base&`, the virtual dispatch mechanism looks at the runtime type of the object, which is `Derived`, and calls `Derived::hello`, printing `Derived`.

`speak` is NOT marked `virtual` in `Animal`. With a non-virtual function, the compiler resolves the call using the static type of the pointer, which is `Animal*`. So `Animal::speak` is called and `Animal` is printed, even though the object is really a `Cat`.

prompt_md: A base class declares `virtual void draw() const`. A derived class defines `void draw()` (without `const`). What happens when `draw` is called through a `Base*` pointing to a `Derived` object?

options.a: The derived `draw()` is called because the function name matches.
options.b: A compile error is generated because the signatures differ.
options.c: The base `draw()` is called because the missing `const` means the derived version is NOT an override.

explanation_md: A function only overrides a virtual base function if its full signature matches, including `const` qualification. Dropping `const` creates a separate, unrelated function in `Derived`. The virtual dispatch mechanism finds no override and calls the base version. Using `override` (lesson 25.3) would make the compiler flag this mismatch.

options.a: `Dog::speak` must be declared `virtual` — only derived classes need the keyword.
options.b: `Animal::speak` is not declared `virtual`, so the call dispatches on the static type of the pointer (`Animal*`), ignoring the runtime type.
options.c: The pointer `p` must be a `Dog*` for virtual dispatch to work.
options.d: `speak` must return a value for polymorphism to apply.

explanation_md: Virtual dispatch is triggered only when the base class declares the function `virtual`. Without `virtual` on `Animal::speak`, the compiler uses the declared type of `p` (`Animal*`) to resolve the call at compile time. Marking `Animal::speak` as `virtual` fixes the issue.

prompt_md: Which statement about virtual functions in C++ is true?

options.a: Only the most-derived class in a hierarchy needs to declare a function `virtual`.
options.b: Once a function is declared `virtual` in a base class, all overrides in derived classes are automatically virtual too.
options.c: A virtual function must always be pure (= 0) to enable polymorphism.
options.d: Virtual dispatch works only with pointers, not references.

explanation_md: In C++, once a function is declared `virtual` in a base class, any function in a derived class with the same name and signature is implicitly virtual as well — even without the `virtual` keyword in the derived class. Virtual dispatch works through both pointers and references (option d is wrong). Pure virtual functions (option c) are a separate concept for abstract classes.
